title: The tofu product marketed as "the healthy human flesh alternative"
author: Ellsworth Toohey
summary: In May 2005, a Tuck School of Business student named Mark Nuckols launched Hufu, a tofu product marketed as "the healthy human flesh alternative for the cannibalism-curious." The origin story: Nuckols was eating a tofurkey sandwich while reading Marvin Harris's anthropology book on cannibalism when the idea struck him. —
full_text:
In May 2005, a Tuck School of Business student named Mark Nuckols launched Hufu, a tofu product marketed as "the healthy human flesh alternative for the cannibalism-curious." The origin story: Nuckols was eating a tofurkey sandwich while reading Marvin Harris's anthropology book on cannibalism when the idea struck him. He made it real.

The site ran for just over a year, from May 2005 to June 2006, long enough to be covered by The Harvard Crimson, The Stanford Daily, and in a Daily Show segment with Samantha Bee. Actress Milla Jovovich allegedly coined the name "Hufu" after hearing about the product on a Eurostar train, a detail that seems implausible yet fits perfectly with the product's spirit.

The debate that actually followed Hufu online was whether it was a joke or a genuine attempt to give cannibalism-curious people a legal alternative — a question that says more about the internet of 2005 than about Nuckols's actual intentions. He eventually resolved it by shutting the site down: "The world has moved on past hufu, and the site was more expensive to run than it was worth."
